speaker: JULIA
text: But say, Lucetta, now we are alone,

speaker: JULIA
text: Wouldst thou then counsel me to fall in love?

speaker: LUCETTA
text: Ay, madam, so you stumble not unheedfully.

speaker: JULIA
text: Of all the fair resort of gentlemen

speaker: JULIA
text: That every day with parle encounter me,

speaker: JULIA
text: In thy opinion which is worthiest love?

speaker: LUCETTA
text: Please you repeat their names, I’ll show my mind

speaker: LUCETTA
text: According to my shallow simple skill.

speaker: JULIA
text: What think’st thou of the fair Sir Eglamour?

speaker: LUCETTA
text: As of a knight well-spoken, neat, and fine;

speaker: LUCETTA
text: But, were I you, he never should be mine.

speaker: JULIA
text: What think’st thou of the rich Mercatio?

speaker: LUCETTA
text: Well of his wealth, but of himself so-so.

speaker: JULIA
text: What think’st thou of the gentle Proteus?

speaker: LUCETTA
text: Lord, Lord, to see what folly reigns in us!

speaker: JULIA
text: How now? What means this passion at his name?

speaker: LUCETTA
text: Pardon, dear madam, ’tis a passing shame

speaker: LUCETTA
text: That I, unworthy body as I am,

speaker: LUCETTA
text: Should censure thus on lovely gentlemen.

speaker: JULIA
text: Why not on Proteus, as of all the rest?

speaker: LUCETTA
text: Then thus: of many good, I think him best.

speaker: JULIA
text: Your reason?

speaker: LUCETTA
text: I have no other but a woman’s reason:

speaker: LUCETTA
text: I think him so because I think him so.

speaker: JULIA
text: And wouldst thou have me cast my love on him?

speaker: LUCETTA
text: Ay, if you thought your love not cast away.

speaker: JULIA
text: Why, he of all the rest hath never moved me.

speaker: LUCETTA
text: Yet he of all the rest I think best loves you.

speaker: JULIA
text: His little speaking shows his love but small.

speaker: LUCETTA
text: Fire that’s closest kept burns most of all.

speaker: JULIA
text: They do not love that do not show their love.

speaker: LUCETTA
text: O, they love least that let men know their love.

speaker: JULIA
text: I would I knew his mind.

speaker: LUCETTA
text: Peruse this paper, madam.

speaker: JULIA
text: “To Julia.”—Say from whom.

speaker: LUCETTA
text: That the contents will show.

speaker: JULIA
text: Say, say who gave it thee.

speaker: LUCETTA
text: Sir Valentine’s page; and sent, I think, from Proteus.

speaker: LUCETTA
text: He would have given it you, but I, being in the way,

speaker: LUCETTA
text: Did in your name receive it. Pardon the fault, I pray.

speaker: JULIA
text: Now, by my modesty, a goodly broker!

speaker: JULIA
text: Dare you presume to harbor wanton lines?

speaker: JULIA
text: To whisper and conspire against my youth?

speaker: JULIA
text: Now trust me, ’tis an office of great worth,

speaker: JULIA
text: And you an officer fit for the place.

speaker: JULIA
text: There, take the paper; see it be returned,

speaker: JULIA
text: Or else return no more into my sight.

speaker: LUCETTA
text: To plead for love deserves more fee than hate.

speaker: JULIA
text: Will you be gone?

speaker: LUCETTA
text: That you may ruminate.

speaker: JULIA
text: And yet I would I had o’erlooked the letter.

speaker: JULIA
text: It were a shame to call her back again

speaker: JULIA
text: And pray her to a fault for which I chid her.

speaker: JULIA
text: What fool is she that knows I am a maid

speaker: JULIA
text: And would not force the letter to my view,

speaker: JULIA
text: Since maids in modesty say “no” to that

speaker: JULIA
text: Which they would have the profferer construe “ay”!

speaker: JULIA
text: Fie, fie, how wayward is this foolish love

speaker: JULIA
text: That like a testy babe will scratch the nurse

speaker: JULIA
text: And presently, all humbled, kiss the rod!

speaker: JULIA
text: How churlishly I chid Lucetta hence,

speaker: JULIA
text: When willingly I would have had her here!

speaker: JULIA
text: How angerly I taught my brow to frown,

speaker: JULIA
text: When inward joy enforced my heart to smile!

speaker: JULIA
text: My penance is to call Lucetta back

speaker: JULIA
text: And ask remission for my folly past.—

speaker: JULIA
text: What ho, Lucetta!

speaker: LUCETTA
text: What would your Ladyship?

speaker: JULIA
text: Is ’t near dinner time?

speaker: LUCETTA
text: I would it were,

speaker: LUCETTA
text: That you might kill your stomach on your meat

speaker: LUCETTA
text: And not upon your maid.

speaker: JULIA
text: What is ’t that you took up so gingerly?

speaker: LUCETTA
text: Nothing.

speaker: JULIA
text: Why didst thou stoop, then?

speaker: LUCETTA
text: To take a paper up that I let fall.

speaker: JULIA
text: And is that paper nothing?

speaker: LUCETTA
text: Nothing concerning me.

speaker: JULIA
text: Then let it lie for those that it concerns.

speaker: LUCETTA
text: Madam, it will not lie where it concerns

speaker: LUCETTA
text: Unless it have a false interpreter.

speaker: JULIA
text: Some love of yours hath writ to you in rhyme.

speaker: LUCETTA
text: That I might sing it, madam, to a tune,

speaker: LUCETTA
text: Give me a note. Your Ladyship can set—

speaker: JULIA
text: As little by such toys as may be possible.

speaker: JULIA
text: Best sing it to the tune of “Light o’ Love.”

speaker: LUCETTA
text: It is too heavy for so light a tune.

speaker: JULIA
text: Heavy? Belike it hath some burden then?

speaker: LUCETTA
text: Ay, and melodious were it, would you sing it.

speaker: JULIA
text: And why not you?

speaker: LUCETTA
text: I cannot reach so high.

speaker: JULIA
text: Let’s see your song. How now, minion!

speaker: LUCETTA
text: Keep tune there still, so you will sing it out.

speaker: LUCETTA
text: And yet methinks I do not like this tune.

speaker: JULIA
text: You do not?

speaker: LUCETTA
text: No, madam, ’tis too sharp.

speaker: JULIA
text: You, minion, are too saucy.

speaker: LUCETTA
text: Nay, now you are too flat

speaker: LUCETTA
text: And mar the concord with too harsh a descant.

speaker: LUCETTA
text: There wanteth but a mean to fill your song.

speaker: JULIA
text: The mean is drowned with your unruly bass.

speaker: LUCETTA
text: Indeed, I bid the base for Proteus.

speaker: JULIA
text: This babble shall not henceforth trouble me.

speaker: JULIA
text: Here is a coil with protestation.

speaker: JULIA
text: Go, get you gone, and let the papers lie.

speaker: JULIA
text: You would be fing’ring them to anger me.

speaker: LUCETTA
text: She makes it strange, but she would be best pleased

speaker: LUCETTA
text: To be so angered with another letter.

speaker: JULIA
text: Nay, would I were so angered with the same!

speaker: JULIA
text: O hateful hands, to tear such loving words!

speaker: JULIA
text: Injurious wasps, to feed on such sweet honey

speaker: JULIA
text: And kill the bees that yield it with your stings!

speaker: JULIA
text: I’ll kiss each several paper for amends.

speaker: JULIA
text: Look, here is writ “kind Julia.” Unkind Julia,

speaker: JULIA
text: As in revenge of thy ingratitude,

speaker: JULIA
text: I throw thy name against the bruising stones,

speaker: JULIA
text: Trampling contemptuously on thy disdain.

speaker: JULIA
text: And here is writ “love-wounded Proteus.”

speaker: JULIA
text: Poor wounded name, my bosom as a bed

speaker: JULIA
text: Shall lodge thee till thy wound be throughly healed,

speaker: JULIA
text: And thus I search it with a sovereign kiss.

speaker: JULIA
text: But twice or thrice was “Proteus” written down.

speaker: JULIA
text: Be calm, good wind. Blow not a word away

speaker: JULIA
text: Till I have found each letter in the letter

speaker: JULIA
text: Except mine own name. That some whirlwind bear

speaker: JULIA
text: Unto a ragged, fearful, hanging rock

speaker: JULIA
text: And throw it thence into the raging sea.

speaker: JULIA
text: Lo, here in one line is his name twice writ:

speaker: JULIA
text: “Poor forlorn Proteus, passionate Proteus,

speaker: JULIA
text: To the sweet Julia.” That I’ll tear away—

speaker: JULIA
text: And yet I will not, sith so prettily

speaker: JULIA
text: He couples it to his complaining names.

speaker: JULIA
text: Thus will I fold them one upon another.

speaker: JULIA
text: Now kiss, embrace, contend, do what you will.

speaker: LUCETTA
text: Madam, dinner is ready, and your father stays.

speaker: JULIA
text: Well, let us go.

speaker: LUCETTA
text: What, shall these papers lie like telltales here?

speaker: JULIA
text: If you respect them, best to take them up.

speaker: LUCETTA
text: Nay, I was taken up for laying them down.

speaker: LUCETTA
text: Yet here they shall not lie, for catching cold.

speaker: JULIA
text: I see you have a month’s mind to them.

speaker: LUCETTA
text: Ay, madam, you may say what sights you see;

speaker: LUCETTA
text: I see things too, although you judge I wink.

speaker: JULIA
text: Come, come, will ’t please you go?
